title: Necessive Structure
titleEn: Must Do / Have To - Necessity Expressions
description: Express what you MUST do or HAVE TO do: Genitive + Must verb + Basic form verb

questions.text: What is the basic formula for the necessive structure?
options: Genitive + must verb + basic form verb
questions.explanation: Formula: Genitive + must verb (täytyy/pitää/on pakko) + basic form (infinitive). Example: 'Mun täytyy mennä' (I must go).

questions.text: List the genitive forms of personal pronouns for the necessive structure: I, you, he/she, we, you plural, they.
questions.explanation: Genitive forms: mun (I), sun (you), hänen (he/she), meidän (we), teidän (you plural), heiden (they). These are the subject forms for necessive sentences.

questions.text: Write: 'Lisa must buy a phone.' Use proper genitive form with a name.
questions.explanation: Name Lisa → genitive Lisan. 'Liisan täytyy ostaa puhelin.' The second verb 'ostaa' stays in basic form, never conjugated.

questions.text: Which of these is NOT a valid 'must' verb in the necessive structure?
options: joutuu (to be forced to - different structure)
questions.explanation: 'Joutuu' is close but uses a different structure. The three main necessive verbs are: täytyy, pitää, and on pakko. All mean the same thing.

questions.text: Express 'I must go' in three different ways using three different must verbs.
questions.explanation: All three are correct and mean the same: 1) Mun täytyy mennä 2) Mun pitää mennä 3) Mun on pakko mennä. Choose whichever feels natural!

questions.text: What happens to the second verb in a necessive sentence?
options: It gets conjugated with personal endings
It stays in basic form (infinitive), never conjugated
questions.explanation: The second verb NEVER changes: 'Mun täytyy mennä' (I must go), 'Sun täytyy ostaa' (You must buy), 'Hän täytyy lukea' (He must read).

questions.text: How do you say 'You don't have to leave' in Finnish?
questions.explanation: In NEGATIVE, you must use 'ei tarvitse' (not 'ei täytyy'): 'Sun ei tarvitse lähteä' = You don't have to leave. This is a fixed structure!

questions.text: Write two sentences: (1) 'I must study' and (2) 'I don't have to study'
questions.explanation: Positive: 'Mun täytyy opiskella' (I must study). Negative: 'Mun ei tarvitse opiskella' (I don't have to study). Note the change: täytyy → ei tarvitse.

questions.text: How do you ask 'Do you have to go?' in Finnish?
questions.explanation: Add -ko/-kö to the must verb: 'Täytyykö sun mennä?' = Do you have to go? The main verb stays in basic form.

questions.text: Ask: 'Do we have to wait?' in Finnish.
questions.explanation: All three questions are valid: 1) Täytyykö meidän odottaa? 2) Pitääkö meidän odottaa? 3) Onko meidän pakko odottaa?

questions.text: What does 'kannattaa' mean in the context of a sentence like 'Sun kannattaa lukea tää kirja'?
options: You should read this book / It's worth reading (recommendation)
questions.explanation: 'Kannattaa' means 'is worth doing / you should.' It's softer than täytyy—a recommendation, not an obligation. Same structure: genitive + kannattaa + basic form.

questions.text: Write: 'You should watch this movie.' Use 'kannattaa' (worth doing).
questions.explanation: 'Sun kannattaa katsoa tää elokuva' = You should watch this movie (it's worth it). Softer than: 'Sun täytyy katsoa tää elokuva' (you must).

questions.text: What is the main difference between 'täytyy', 'pitää', and 'kannattaa'?
options: They all mean exactly the same thing
Only täytyy is correct, others are wrong
questions.explanation: täytyy/pitää express obligation (MUST). kannattaa is softer, expressing recommendations (SHOULD). All use the same structure.

questions.text: Create a mini dialogue. Person A asks if Person B has to go. Person B answers yes and explains where they must go.
questions.explanation: Example: A: 'Täytyykö sun mennä?' B: 'Joo, mun täytyy mennä kauppaan' (Yes, I must go to the store). The second sentence doesn't need to repeat the structure completely.
